title: Entanglement distribution protocols under imperfect fidelity and quantum memory conditions
authors: Claire Mesny, Fabrice Guillemin, Claire Goursaud
abstract: The rapid development of quantum computers and sensors urges for the development of a quantum Internet capable of transmitting quantum bits over long distances. Photons used for quantum data transfer are fragile over time and sensitive to their environment, so that they cannot be directly used over long distances. To remedy this problem, long distance paths are segmented into shorter links and entangled pairs of photons are distributed over these links and swapped to create end-to-end entangled pairs over long distances, eventually used for teleportation. In this paper, we develop an existing protocol taking account of fidelity and imperfect memories. We shorten the execution time and thus increase its link success probability creating the so-called Locally Heralded Distribution (LHD). It turns out that the proposed protocol outperforms some previous protocols. We benchmark through simulation the performances of protocols considered in this paper by using a blind entanglement protocol as a baseline.
summary: This paper proposes and analyzes the Locally Heralded Distribution (LHD) protocol for generating long-distance entanglement over quantum repeaters. By incorporating realistic noise models for quantum memory and fiber loss, the authors demonstrate that LHD significantly improves upon existing protocols like Blind Swapping, making it a more viable candidate for future quantum internet infrastructure.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of establishing long-distance quantum entanglement necessary for a quantum Internet, specifically accounting for photon fragility and imperfect quantum memory.
main_result: The proposed Locally Heralded Distribution (LHD) protocol is shown to outperform previous methods (Blind Swapping and Globally Heralded Distribution) by achieving higher link success probabilities.
method: The authors develop and compare several entanglement distribution protocols (BS, GHD, LHD) by deriving formulas for link success probability and end-to-end fidelity under realistic noise models.
model_or_system: The model involves distributing entangled photon pairs over segmented links connected by repeater nodes, utilizing quantum memories whose fidelity and loss are explicitly modeled over time.
key_observables: End-to-end entanglement fidelity, link success probability, and the comparison between different protocol performance metrics.
important_parameters: Memory lifetime ($	au$), coherence time ($	au_{coh}$), fiber attenuation ($\alpha$), and the number of links ($l_r$).
main_assumptions: The analysis incorporates both fiber-induced and memory-induced decoherence, and assumes deterministic write-in/read-out operations for quantum memories.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the path schemes and compare link probabilities (BS, LHD, GHD) and link fidelities as a function of increasing route length ($l_r$).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem, details the physical models for decoherence and loss, develops the LHD protocol, and concludes by benchmarking LHD against established protocols using simulation results.
why_interesting: The explicit modeling of combined fiber loss and memory decoherence, alongside the comparative analysis of repeater protocols, provides crucial theoretical benchmarks for building quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
